description:
You are acting as a monitoring specialist who has just installed the lightweight, file-based alerting tool called “serverwatch” on the current machine.  
  The tool is driven entirely by the plain-text rule file  

    /home/user/monitoring/alert.rules  

  Each line of that file represents a single alert in the following **strict, space-separated, case-sensitive format**:

    ALERT <alert_name> <threshold_percent> <notification_email>

  • alert_name: one word, no spaces  
  • threshold_percent: an integer in the range 1-100 (no “%” sign)  
  • notification_email: a valid e-mail address  
  • No extra spaces before/after the line, no blank lines, no trailing newline.

  Your task: create the directory (if it does not already exist) and add **exactly one** alert line that will trigger when CPU utilisation exceeds 85 %, sending a notification to “ops@example.com”.  

  Important verification requirements:  
  1. The file /home/user/monitoring/alert.rules must exist.  
  2. The file must contain exactly the single line (no leading/trailing whitespace, no newline at the end of file):  
       ALERT cpu_high 85 ops@example.com  
  3. No other files or directories should be created or modified.  
  4. Accomplish everything with a single terminal command (you may combine utilities with “&&” or “;” if needed, but it must be issued in one line).  

  Once done, you can display the file content to confirm, but the automated test will inspect the file directly.
truth:
The testing harness will check:

  • Path: /home/user/monitoring/alert.rules  
  • File permissions: default umask-derived (the agent need not change them).  
  • File content must be exactly 28 bytes long:  
      41 4c 45 52 54 20 63 70 75 5f 68 69 67 68 20 38  
      35 20 6f 70 73 40 65 78 61 6d 70 6c 65 2e 63 6f  
      6d  
    Which, when decoded as UTF-8, yields:  
      ALERT cpu_high 85 ops@example.com  

  No trailing newline (last byte is ‘m’, ASCII 0x6d).  
  No other files under /home/user/monitoring/ apart from alert.rules.
name: task_000000_216b2f73
